title: MKT10009 Lecture 7 Part 2
summary: MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 7 - Chapter 10 – “P” for Place: Customer value fulfilmentPart 2 - The nature of marketing channels and value creation. Chapter 10 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing, 8th edition
content_html:
MKT10009 Marketing and the Consumer ExperienceLecture 7 - Chapter 10 – “P” for Place: Customer value fulfilmentPart 2 - The nature of marketing channels and value creation.
Chapter 10 from our Textbook: Principles of Marketing,
8th edition
Presented by Dr Gordon Campbell

Part 2  - The nature of marketing channels and value creation.

The nature of marketing channels and value creation (1 of 7)
A marketing channel (or distribution channel) is a set of interdependent organisations involved in the process of making a product or service available to users.
Marketing channels involve the interaction of intermediaries to bring finished goods and services to the marketplace.

The nature of marketing channels and value creation (2 of 7)
How channel members add value
Channel members add value by bridging time, place and possession gaps that separate goods and services from those who use them.
Key functions of channel members include:
information
promotion
contact
matching
negotiation
physical distribution
financing
risk taking.
Q: Are any of these functions more important than the others?
Why, or why not?

Figure 10.3
How a distributor reduces the number of channel transactions

The nature of marketing channels and value creation (4 of 7)
Number of channel levels – examples on the next slide
A channel level is a layer of intermediaries who perform some work in bringing the product and its ownership closer to the final buyer.
The number of intermediary levels indicates the length of a channel.
A direct marketing channel has no intermediary levels.
An indirect marketing channel contains one or more intermediary levels.

Figure 10.4
Consumer and business marketing channels
Q: Give an example of a business that might use (a) a direct marketing channel and (b) an indirect marketing channel.

The nature of marketing channels and value creation (6 of 7)
Number of channel levels (cont.)
The institutions in a marketing channel are connected by several types of flows:
product flow
ownership flow
payment flow
information flow
promotion flow.

The nature of marketing channels and value creation (7 of 7)
Channels in the service sector
Producers of services and experiences also use marketing channels to make their output available to target populations.
Q: Describe a marketing channel for a service organisation such as  (a) a school or hospital and (b) a tourism business.

The nature of marketing channels and value creation
A: Farms could sell direct to consumers and also to retailers. Insurance companies can sell direct to consumers through their website and also through insurance brokers. Dulux Paints in Australia sell direct from their own shop to tradespeople, and direct through the Internet to professional painters, and through retailers such as hardware chains.
A: All are essential. The supply chain consists of individual performers operating as one integrated process. The various parts must work effectively together to achieve the overall objective of timely delivery at least cost.
Q: Give an example of a business that might use (a) a direct marketing channel and (b) an indirect marketing channel.
Q: Slide 2 of 7 - Key functions of channel members include…
Are any of these functions more important than the others?
Why, or why not?

The nature of marketing channels and value creation
A: Hospital: some private health insurance companies have “preferred” providers and therefore these insurers are acting as a type of sales agent for the “preferred” hospital. Hospitals have fundraising operations to obtain donations and to encourage people to include a bequest to the hospital in their will. Tourism: tourism operators can have websites where they sell direct to the public and might include links to other accommodation and transport providers. They can also conduct direct marketing activities to potential customers and can participate with other tourism operators in their area in public events such as “Visit (insert name of location)”.
Q: Describe a marketing channel for a service organisation such as  (a) a school or hospital and (b) a tourism business.

Channel behaviour and organization.
Channel design and management decisions.
